Chapter 12: Ruins, Dungeons & Wilderness Locations ​

The Dead Lands' Named Zones, What Expeditions Find There, and How to Run the Ruins at the Table ​

The geographic and political context of the Dead Lands is covered in Chapter 3. What follows is different: specific entries for the ruins that licensed expeditions actually visit, the texture of each named zone at the level of what a party finds on the ground, and the GM's working guide for describing these locations at the table without the prose breaking under the weight of what they actually are.

Chapter 3 establishes that the Chroniclers' named zones — First City, Ner Aak, Kalazym — are not separate ruins but identifiable sections of something much larger: towers protruding above the ice in clusters, connected by subsurface corridors, with open stretches between clusters that read as streets rather than wilderness. This chapter takes that premise as given and works from it. The locations below are described as expeditions experience them: from the outside in, from the accessible surfaces down into the subsurface systems that every expedition eventually reaches and that most expedition leaders advise against entering without preparation you didn't bring.

Approaching the Named Zones ​

Every licensed Dead Lands expedition begins at the Chronicler waystation network — either the Bellarian stations on the western approach or the Norderenn crossing points that have been passable since the occupation, under conditions the Chronicler field personnel describe as cooperative. The approach from either direction produces, before the ruins themselves, a transitional zone that experienced expedition leaders call the Changed Country.

The Changed Country is not a dramatic thing. It is a gradual thing. The snow is deeper than it should be for the season. The wind carries a smell that most parties take two or three days to notice and then cannot stop noticing: cold metal and old water, neither of which belongs to open tundra. The sky above the Dead Lands proper has a quality that field reports describe as overcast in a direction that isn't up. The horizon where Eymanarann begins is not a line of ruins but an absence of the horizon, replaced by something else that becomes identifiable only when the party is close enough for the shapes to resolve.

What the shapes resolve into: towers. Eight to fourteen stories of grey material in clustered groups, the lowest sections buried in the ice at angles that are not quite the angles of natural subsidence, several showing the regular openings of their upper stories above the ice line and the sealed or open faces of their lower sections visible where expedition labor has cleared the surface back. They do not look like anything the Known World has built, and they do not look like ruins — which is the thing that unsettles parties more than the ruins themselves. A ruin has collapsed. These have not collapsed. They have been buried.

The Frostbite River ​

The river that defines the Dead Lands' southern boundary runs along the base of the northern mountain range and feeds into the eastern sea. What it is not is a river that behaves like a river should. Its bed is too regular. Its width is consistent across its full navigable length in a way that no natural river maintains. The stone of its banks is the same grey poured material as the Dead Lands structures, not natural rock, visible where the water has cut into it. In several sections, the riverbed is not stone at all but a material identical to the subsurface corridor flooring documented in the named zones.

The Chroniclers' internal designation for the Frostbite River section along Ner Aak's southern edge is in a field report from 1,299 TE, filed at an access tier that requires direct Chronicler escort to view.

For parties crossing the river to reach the named zones from the south: it is shallow, fordable in summer, passable in winter where it freezes. The cold comes from the water itself rather than the air above it, which is backwards. The experienced guides from the Bellarian waystation communities cross it quickly and do not camp adjacent to it. When asked why, they say it keeps them up.

FIRST CITY ​

Primary access zone. Most expeditions begin and end here. The Chroniclers' most complete maps cover the upper levels. The subsurface systems are mapped to one level below ground, then not mapped.

The towers of First City are the first structures visible from the standard approach and the tallest in the accessible zones. The expedition count of eight to twelve structures is a working figure; the actual count depends on where you draw the distinction between a separate structure and a wing of an existing one, which the ruins refuse to answer simply. At ground level the towers connect. The connections are not corridors between buildings; they are passages through material that continues from one tower to the next without a visible join.

The streets between them are paved in the same grey material as the walls, slightly roughened in a regular pattern, wide enough that four wagons could pass abreast without the parties acknowledging each other. Where the ice has not reclaimed the surface, the paving is intact. The expeditions that have cleared the ice back from stretches of these streets and walked them report the same observation in their field notes, which the Chroniclers have not yet identified as significant: the sound of walking on them is wrong. Not echoey. Not deadened. The sound arrives in the correct place at the correct time, but the space does something to it that stone streets do not.

The interior of First City's accessible sections: long passages with openings on either side at regular intervals, each opening the same size, most sealed flush with the wall by the same grey material. The sealed openings are flush — no seam is visible until you press your hand against them and feel the temperature difference at the edges. The open ones open into spaces approximately eight metres by twelve, with a ceiling at four metres, and on the far wall in most rooms, a dormant flat panel set into the surface at chest height. The panels are perfectly black. They reflect nothing. They have been scraped, struck, heated, and subjected to detect magic by every expedition that has tried to understand them. Detect magic finds nothing. They are cool to the touch.

What the subsurface level contains. The access points are in the floors — openings where the paved surface has been removed or collapsed, leading to a level below the streets. The subsurface level has a lower ceiling, the same uniform grey, and the same regular openings. The difference is the temperature, which drops noticeably, and the sound, which is different again: absorbed rather than present. The passages run straight in both directions past any distance a torch will reach. They have not been mapped to their ends because their ends have not been found.

What the Chroniclers know. The towers of First City carry internal labels in expedition logs filed before 1,330 TE, when the current access tier system was implemented. Those earlier reports name specific structures numerically and describe the connection to the subsurface system as "Transit Node 1 primary approaches — east and west." The designation has not been explained to expedition leaders operating under current access protocols.

GM note: running First City. The productive running principle is scale made personal. Every time the party thinks they understand the size of the place, something extends it: streets that don't end, rooms that continue beyond the expedition's light, passages that go down rather than in. The sound behavior is a useful GM tool — it marks the transition from outdoor ruins to interior of something large enough to have acoustics. Parties will notice it and ask about it. The right answer is honest: you don't know what it was built for, but it was built, and whoever built it knew what sound does in enclosed spaces. The patrol constructs documented in Chapter 1 of Book III use the subsurface corridors as their primary circuit; their activation conditions are covered in those entries. What the GM should add at the table: the party will not know they have triggered something until they are no longer alone in the corridor.

NER AAK ​

The name is Eymanarynn in origin — the most common scholarly interpretation is that it is a place designation, though the language base is too fragmentary for certainty. The Chroniclers use it because a Vartran translator from a 1,289 TE expedition read it from an inscription at the zone's most visible cluster, and the name was easier than inventing one themselves.

Middle access zone. Higher density of intact sealed sections than First City. Lower expedition frequency. Patrol construct activity higher per expedition party than in First City.

Ner Aak is denser. The clusters are spaced more closely, the passages between them narrower, and the subsurface system more immediately accessible — where First City's access points are mostly collapsed or require deliberate expedition labor to open, Ner Aak has sections where the subsurface level is open from ground level, the paving above having given way long enough ago that whatever fell has been displaced. You can see down into the passages from the surface. Several expeditions have reported that when their torchlight reached the right angle, they could see the parallel iron rails running along the floor of the subsurface level — two rails, spaced consistently, set into stone crossties, disappearing into the darkness in both directions. No expedition has followed those rails to where they go.

The Frostbite River's edge is one kilometre south of Ner Aak's primary cluster. The subsurface temperature here is several degrees warmer than in First City, which expedition surveyors attribute to proximity to the river. The attribution satisfies some surveyors and troubles others who know what the river's bed is made of.

The flat panels in Ner Aak are more numerous than in First City, and a small percentage of them are not entirely dormant. The light they produce is faint — detectable in darkness, not in daylight — irregular rather than steady, and closer in color to tallow candles than to fire. Detect magic: nothing. The Chroniclers' most recent expedition to Ner Aak recovered three examples. One is in their restricted archives. One was destroyed in transit. The one the expedition leader kept is the Listening Shard documented in Book III's magic item catalogue.

What expeditions find in depth. The sealed sections of Ner Aak are sealed from the inside in several documented cases — the mechanism by which they were sealed is on the interior side, which is not the side expedition parties approach from. The Warden Discs catalogued in Book III open some of these. The zone each disc opens varies; the Chroniclers' knowledge of which discs correspond to which sections of Ner Aak is one of the practical reasons their expedition guidance is considered worth the licensing fee.

What lives in Ner Aak. The patrol constructs are more active here than in First City, and their trigger sensitivity is less well characterized. Several expedition reports describe activation in response to something the expedition could not subsequently identify. A Chronicler expedition leader who filed two such reports noted, in an addendum to a third, that the triggers appeared responsive to specific uses of magic — not all uses, but specific ones, and in each documented case the activation followed the casting of a divination effect by a party member. The addendum was filed. Expeditions in the years since have not systematically tested the hypothesis.

GM note: running Ner Aak. The transition from First City to Ner Aak is the transition from exploration to investigation. First City can be run as a dungeon that keeps getting larger. Ner Aak requires the party to become students of the place. Things here respond to what they do — the panels, the constructs, the sealed sections that the Warden Disc opens. The GM should pace this as a place with logic rather than a place with dangers; the logic is not friendly, but it is there. Parties that approach Ner Aak carefully — attentive to what changes when they change things — will find more and lose less than parties that treat it as combat terrain. The panel light is a useful running detail: in a location where everything is grey and cold, a faint irregular light in the darkness is something parties pay attention to. What changes when they approach it, what changes when they don't, is information the GM can deliver without commentary.

KALAZYM ​

Deep access zone. The farthest point from the Bellarian waystation network that Chronicler-licensed expeditions are currently authorized to reach. The authorization ceiling has not moved in forty years despite two applications to extend it. The applications are in the restricted archive under "pending."

Kalazym is where the surface ruins end and the subsurface ruins begin in earnest. The tower clusters are lower here — many upper sections have collapsed or been buried under ice shifts — and the ground-level passages are mostly obstructed. The accessible route is down. The subsurface level is larger than in the other zones: passages four metres wide, higher ceilings, the same rail formations on the floor, and in several sections a second track running parallel to the first at consistent spacing. Where the two-track sections are, the crossties between the rails are more substantial and the floor between the tracks is smooth, different in material from the floor on either side.

The temperature at depth in Kalazym is the anomaly that expedition leaders spend the most time not explaining in their reports. It is warmer the deeper you go. Not dramatically warmer, but measurably and consistently warmer in a way that cannot be accounted for by proximity to the river or by the insulating property of the ice above. Several of the deeper passages maintain warmth across all seasons.

Certain corridors at depth carry a low vibration. It is not constant — it appears and disappears over periods that Chronicler monitoring expeditions have established are not random. The pattern has been documented but not published. The internal notation in the monitoring files: see Heartbeat project, restricted.

What the deepest accessed level contains. The expedition that reached the third subsurface level in 1,367 TE found chambers with sealed double-width openings on both walls — larger than the standard sealed sections of the upper levels, designed for something the expedition could not infer from the dimensions alone. Several of these openings have been forced. The force was applied from the interior side. The Chroniclers have not authorized a follow-up expedition to the third level.

What the Surveyor's Plate shows in Kalazym. The artifact documented in Book III's magic item catalogue becomes fully active in Kalazym's second subsurface level, and at the third level it begins showing passages the party cannot yet reach — including a convergence of multiple corridor lines at a point the expedition's cartographer estimated four hundred metres northeast of their deepest position. The Plate's behavior in this zone is what the Order of Magnus's research summary describes as "active in a manner inconsistent with any passive artifact." They have not disclosed this to the Chroniclers, who are seeking the Plate.

GM note: running Kalazym. This is where the setting's logic becomes felt rather than discovered. The descent into Kalazym should produce a specific sensation: the party is going into something that is more operational the deeper they go, not less. The warmth is the first signal. The vibration is the second. The Surveyor's Plate, if the party has it, makes the third unmissable. The right running question at depth is not "what is trying to kill them?" but "what is this place still doing?" The patrol constructs here are at maximum density; the chamber-bound guardians documented in Book III appear on the third level. The sealed double-width openings that were forced from the interior are a detail to deliver without comment and let stand. Whatever came through them is somewhere in the third level. The party's awareness of this precedes any encounter with it, which is the correct order.

The Deep Roads ​

The Chroniclers' public maps of the Dead Lands do not show the full extent of the subsurface corridor system. The restricted maps show more. What neither set shows — because the information has not been assembled into a single document where its implications would be visible — is that the corridor system connects not only the three named zones of Eymanarann but the underground stations at Zyzak and Dymoras in the east, the passage below the Frostfall Lift in Norderenn, and the foundations of at least two structures in the Bellarian capital whose cellars have never been fully surveyed.

What the corridors are, in the terms of what a party actually navigates: straight passages, wide, running to distances that exceed any expedition's ability to follow them to their ends. The paired rail formations on the floor. Platform-like elevated surfaces at intervals along the walls, each with consistent dimensions and an access point behind it leading to sealed sections. The ceilings carry fixtures at regular intervals — mounted devices of the Vysandri alloy, sealed, in most sections entirely dormant. In several documented stretches, these fixtures emit the same faint irregular light as the panels in Ner Aak.

The system is not dead. The Chroniclers know this because the Bal'mekh stations — Zyzak and Dymoras — have been documented as partially functional in ways that no expedition has been able to fully characterize or shut down. The vibration recorded at depth in Kalazym is consistent, in frequency and pattern, with the vibration recorded at the Zyzak station in 1,351 TE. The correspondence has been noted. Nobody has been assigned to investigate it.

GM note: the Deep Roads. The Deep Roads are the connective tissue of everything the Dead Lands contains. Every named zone above is connected to every other through this system, and the system connects outward to the Known World through access points that are not all on the Chroniclers' maps. A party that finds the Deep Roads has found a route that bypasses every surface checkpoint the Chroniclers maintain. The Chroniclers know this is theoretically possible. They do not know it has been done. Several of the missing expedition members in the past thirty years' records are people who went down and did not come back up in the direction they descended. The Chroniclers have not connected these disappearances to the corridor system in any document currently in circulation.

Running the Dead Lands ​

The setting's register for these locations is a specific discipline. What breaks it is vocabulary — using words that belong to a different world for a location that the characters have no framework for.

What works instead: describe what the characters experience, and let the GM and the attentive player assemble the interpretation. A wall that is smooth in a way stone walls are not. A passage that runs straight past the edge of the light and does not curve. Iron rails laid in parallel pairs on stone crossties, disappearing into darkness in both directions. A flat black panel at chest height that reflects nothing. A smell like cold water in a place where there is no water. A low vibration, felt through the boots, that begins and ends without identifiable cause. None of these descriptions name what the thing is. All of them are accurate to what Third Era characters would experience. The interpretation belongs to the reader of the book, not to the characters within it.

The one vocabulary failure that recurs in expedition accounts, and that GMs should actively avoid at the table: ancient. Everything in the Dead Lands is ancient. Saying so is not description; it is a substitute for description. "The walls have been here longer than any institution in the Known World" is not the same sentence as "the walls are smooth in a way that stone walls are not and the edge where two sections join is not a join you can see or feel." The second version tells the party something. The first version tells them nothing they did not already know.

The Dead Lands do not have an atmosphere. They have specifics. The specifics accumulate into something the party feels without being able to say what it is. That accumulation is the GM's goal, and it is achieved one detail at a time, never by announcing that the place is strange.

Two useful running questions to return to at each stage of an expedition:

What changes when they get closer? The panels that appear dormant from a distance are not warm from a distance. The vibration in the floor of Kalazym's deeper corridors is not felt above a certain depth. The parallel rails are parallel — that geometry is detectable before the rails themselves are legible in torchlight. Giving the party information that resolves into understanding on approach rewards attention without scripting discovery.

What does the party's presence change? The Listening Shard emits more frequently in the Dead Lands than outside it. The patrol constructs respond to specific actions. Sealed sections open to specific items. The Surveyor's Plate updates in response to where the party goes. The Dead Lands are not static backdrop; they are a system that responds to the party's presence. Parties that notice this are parties that are actually inside the setting. That is the experience the Dead Lands locations are designed to produce.
